name: Cinematic Camera
description: A directed camera mode that uses authored shots, cuts, blends, and control locks to frame scripted or dramatic moments.
design_notes: Cinematic camera design should define cinematic_shot_id ownership, timeline triggers, blend profiles, player-control locks, skip behavior, subtitle sync, and restore rules. The camera must communicate drama without hiding required gameplay reads or leaving the player in a bad camera state after interruption.
design_purpose: Frame story, traversal, combat, or reveal moments with authored composition.
Temporarily shift camera priority from player control to a shot timeline or scripted event.
Coordinate camera cuts, subtitles, animation, and gameplay locks without losing restore state.
player_fantasy: The player feels the game is deliberately directing attention to an important moment while preserving orientation afterward.

parameters.name: cinematic_shot_id
parameters.description: Authored shot record selected by the camera director.

parameters.name: shot_timeline_id
parameters.description: Timeline that coordinates camera, animation, subtitles, and events.

parameters.name: camera_blend_profile_id
parameters.description: Blend profile used when entering or exiting the shot.

parameters.name: camera_transition_ms
parameters.description: Milliseconds allocated for the camera blend.

parameters.name: player_control_lock_policy_id
parameters.description: Player input policy while the cinematic camera owns priority.

parameters.name: restore_camera_mode_id
parameters.description: Camera mode restored after skip, completion, or checkpoint reload.

edge_cases: A cinematic starts while the player is in lock-on, split screen, photo mode, or a multiplayer spectator camera.
Skipping the shot must restore camera mode, input locks, subtitles, and pending gameplay triggers exactly once.
Save/load during a cinematic needs either a safe replay point or restored gameplay camera state.
Subtitle timing and camera cuts can drift if the timeline is paused, skipped, or localized voice length changes.
common_bugs: Camera priority remains on the cinematic shot after the sequence ends or is skipped.
A cut triggers twice and fires the same gameplay event or subtitle cue twice.
Player control unlocks before the camera restores collision and occlusion handling.
Checkpoint restore places the player after the cut but the camera before the cut.
balancing_notes: Cinematic shots should be brief or skippable when repeated after failure.
Camera control locks are acceptable for story emphasis but risky during active gameplay hazards.
Important combat or platforming information should not appear only during an unrepeatable camera cut.
accessibility_notes: Respect reduced-motion settings for handheld sway, rapid cuts, field-of-view shifts, and shake layered into the shot.
Keep subtitles and speaker labels synchronized with cinematic timing, including skip and pause behavior.

implementation_notes.unity: Use Cinemachine virtual cameras or a custom director keyed by cinematic_shot_id and camera_priority. Timeline signals should request gameplay locks and subtitle events, while the camera system stores restore_camera_mode_id before taking priority.
implementation_notes.godot: Model cinematic shots as Resources or AnimationPlayer timelines with camera anchors and restore state. On skip or reload, clear input locks, stop the timeline, and restore the previous camera mode in one transaction.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use Level Sequence or Camera Cut tracks with explicit shot ids, blend settings, and player control policies. Store restore state before possession or view-target changes, and handle skip through a single sequence-completion path.
implementation_notes.web: Represent cinematic camera state as a camera director record with shot id, blend ticks, and restore mode. Canvas, Phaser, or Three.js render layers should read committed camera transforms after timeline events resolve.

example_games.title: God of War
example_games.note: Uses directed camera composition during story and combat transitions.

example_games.title: Uncharted 4
example_games.note: Uses cinematic camera moments to frame traversal and narrative scenes.
